region_of_origin: Tropical regions; widely used in Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Caribbean

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for desiccated coconut as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed plant foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of desiccated coconut is approximately 1 tablespoon (10 g), commonly used as a topping, ingredient in baked goods, or garnish in Southeast Asian and South Asian desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Desiccated Coconut — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for desiccated coconut (cr_id F04030017). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Coconut meat, desiccated, unsweetened' (FDC ID 170169) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; USDA reports saturated fat ~57.2 g/100g for unsweetened desiccated coconut, consistent with the total fat of 64.53 g/100g reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for desiccated coconut was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on the analogous food 'coconut meat / coconut products' referenced in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The very high dietary fibre content (16.3 g/100g) and high fat content (64.53 g/100g) strongly attenuate glucose response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 10 g serving calculated using total carbohydrate basis: (45 × 23.65 × 10) / 10000 = 1.06 ≈ 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (37 mg/100g) and high potassium (543 mg/100g) with a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Desiccated coconut is suitable for hypertension management in small portions (10–20 g) due to its very low sodium and high potassium content; however, the very high saturated fat content (~57 g/100g) may contribute to arterial stiffness at large intakes, so use as a flavouring ingredient rather than a main food component.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (16.3 g/100g) and high fat content attenuate glucose absorption; estimated low GI (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Desiccated coconut has a low estimated glycaemic index (~45) and very high fibre (16.3 g/100g) that significantly moderates postprandial glucose response; limit portions to 1–2 tablespoons (10–20 g) per serving to avoid excess caloric intake from its very high energy density (660 kcal/100g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~57 g/100g), predominantly lauric and myristic acids from coconut, raises LDL cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Desiccated coconut is high concern for cardiovascular disease due to its very high saturated fat content (~57 g/100g), which raises LDL cholesterol; restrict to occasional use in very small amounts (≤10 g) and do not use as a primary ingredient in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high coconut-derived saturated fat (~57 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Desiccated coconut is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis management due to its very high coconut saturated fat (~57 g/100g), which is excluded from the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out and promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; avoid or limit to ≤5 g per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Desiccated coconut is the dried, shredded or grated flesh of mature coconuts (Cocos nucifera) from which most moisture has been removed, leaving fine white strands with a dry, crispy texture and a concentrated sweet coconut flavour. It is produced by grating fresh coconut meat and then drying it at low temperature to reduce water content to approximately 3 g/100g. The result is a shelf-stable, calorie-dense ingredient (660 kcal/100g) used extensively in Southeast Asian, South Asian, Caribbean, and Western baking traditions. Nutritionally, desiccated coconut is dominated by fat (64.53 g/100g), of which approximately 57 g/100g is saturated fat — predominantly medium-chain lauric acid and myristic acid — making it one of the highest saturated-fat plant foods available. It also provides notable dietary fibre (16.3 g/100g), moderate protein (6.88 g/100g), and useful minerals including potassium (543 mg/100g), iron (3.32 mg/100g), and calcium (26 mg/100g). Sodium is very low at 37 mg/100g. Clinically, the high fibre and fat content attenuate glucose absorption, giving it a low estimated glycaemic index (~45), making it suitable in small portions for people with type 2 diabetes. However, the very high saturated fat content raises LDL cholesterol and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines, making it a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Common uses include as a topping for kueh and ondeh-ondeh in Singapore and Malaysia, as an ingredient in Indian sweets (ladoo, barfi), in Western coconut cakes and macaroons, and as a garnish in Thai and Indonesian desserts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in desiccated coconut?
Is desiccated coconut good for diabetes?
Is desiccated coconut high in saturated fat?
Can I eat desiccated coconut if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between desiccated coconut and coconut flakes?
How much fibre is in desiccated coconut?
What is the glycemic index of desiccated coconut?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04030017, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170169 (coconut meat, desiccated, unsweetened), which is an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) due to absence of a direct GI measurement for desiccated coconut.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (57.2 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.73 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.71 g/100g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170169 (coconut meat, desiccated, unsweetened) as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions; values are consistent with the total fat of 64.53 g/100g reported by HPB (57.2 + 2.73 + 0.71 = 60.64 g; residual ~3.9 g attributed to other fatty acid fractions not separately reported). 2. trans_fat_g is naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central for unsweetened desiccated coconut. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food containing no vitamin B12. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central for desiccated coconut. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this entry (cr_id F04030017), and comprehensive validated data for all these fields was not available from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170169 without risk of cross-source inconsistency. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (~45, low) based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for desiccated coconut was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 7. The input record used the spelling 'Dessicated' (double-s); the canonical English spelling is 'Desiccated' (single-s); both spellings are preserved in aliases. 8. dietary_tags 'wholegrain' from input removed as desiccated coconut is not a grain product; 'plant_based', 'low_sodium', 'high_fibre', 'high_fat_warning' retained as accurate. 9. No local-language Chinese name included as desiccated coconut is a generic processed ingredient without a single canonical Chinese dish identity requiring mandatory Chinese-script coverage under G6 (G6 mandatory multilingual naming applies to Singaporean dishes; this is a generic processed ingredient record). 10. Aliases consistency self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — 'Desiccated Coconut', 'Dessicated coconut', 'Dried coconut', 'Shredded coconut', 'Coconut flakes (fine)', 'Coconut powder (shredded)', 'Kelapa parut kering', 'தேங்காய் துருவல்', 'नारियल बुरादा', 'Tengai thuruval', 'Nariyal burada' all confirmed present in flat aliases.
